claim: Subagents keep your main context window clean by providing fresh, isolated chat sessions for delegated work, preventing context pollution when processing large amounts of data or files.
actionable: When handling research, file processing, or large data operations, delegate to subagents rather than keeping everything in your main session to preserve context for orchestration.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People

claim: Subagents and skills are functionally identical markdown files with YAML front matter; the distinction is primarily that subagents run in parallel with fresh context while skills execute in the main session.
actionable: Use subagents when you need independent parallel execution with fresh context; use skills for helper workflows that need main session context access.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People

claim: Progressive disclosure via YAML front matter (name, description) allows Claude Code to decide which subagents to invoke without reading full instructions, saving tokens and preventing unnecessary tool invocation.
actionable: Write precise, specific subagent descriptions in YAML front matter that trigger invocation only when relevant; iterate descriptions based on when subagents fail to fire or fire incorrectly.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People

claim: Subagents can be configured as read-only by disallowing file write/edit tools and specifying only read-capable tools, creating permission boundaries that prevent unintended data modification.
actionable: For research or analysis subagents that should not modify code/files, explicitly set read-only tools in the YAML configuration and avoid prompting-based restrictions.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People

claim: Cost optimization pattern: delegate resource-intensive tasks (like reading 300-page documents) to cheaper Haiku subagents instead of Opus, then have the main Opus orchestrator process summaries.
actionable: Design subagent teams where Opus/Sonnet handles orchestration and complex reasoning while Haiku specializes in document reading, summarization, and other high-volume work.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People

claim: Subagents wake up with fresh context by default, allowing them to provide unbiased reviews or analysis without being influenced by previous conversation context.
actionable: When you need objective, unbiased critique of work (code reviews, plan reviews), use dedicated subagents that start with zero memory instead of continuing in main session.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People

claim: Subagents should be specialized rather than generic—better to have one agent that excels at security auditing, one at database design, one at documentation than one agent trying to do everything.
actionable: Design your subagent team as an assembly line of specialists; assign each subagent deep subject matter expertise in one domain rather than broad generalist capabilities.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People

claim: Skip subagents for single quick edits, sequential dependent tasks, or cases where the subagent needs full conversation context; delegate when dumping large piles of output you won't reread or running parallel independent work.
actionable: Before creating a subagent, ask: 'Will this dump a pile of data I never reread?' If yes, use a subagent. If tasks are interdependent or brief, keep work in main session.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People

claim: Subagents cannot talk to each other; they have one-to-one relationships with the main orchestrator session, not many-to-many communication.
actionable: If you need multiple agents to coordinate with each other, use agent teams instead of subagents; for independent parallel work only, subagents are sufficient.
source_title: How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People
